title: Best, Average, Worst, Expected
subtitle: The same algorithm has different complexities depending on input

sections.heading: One algorithm, many complexities
sections.body:
When we say 'quicksort is O(n log n)', that's almost true. Quicksort is O(n²) on already-sorted input. Linear search is O(1) if your target is the very first element. The complexity depends on what input you give it.

We distinguish four flavors:

* **Best case** — the friendliest input. Often a misleading number.
* **Worst case** — the most hostile input. The safety guarantee.
* **Average case** — the expected behavior over 'typical' inputs.
* **Expected case** — for randomized algorithms, averaged over the algorithm's own coin flips.

Most of the time, when an engineer says 'big O', they mean **worst case**.

sections.heading: Worst-case is the safety guarantee
sections.body:
Why do most people default to worst-case? Because it's a *promise*. If you say 'this algorithm is O(n²) in the worst case', no input on Earth can make it slower than that (up to constants).

Best case is rarely useful because the universe doesn't always serve up the friendliest input. Saying 'my sort is O(n) in the best case (already-sorted)' is technically true and almost never relevant.

data.text:
Algorithm           | Best       | Average    | Worst      | Practical?
  --------------------+------------+------------+------------+------------
  Linear search       | O(1)       | O(n)       | O(n)       | yes, simple
  Binary search       | O(1)       | O(log n)   | O(log n)   | yes, needs sorted
  Bubble sort         | O(n)       | O(n²)      | O(n²)      | no, demo only
  Insertion sort      | O(n)       | O(n²)      | O(n²)      | yes, for small n
  Merge sort          | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | yes, always
  Quick sort          | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n²)*     | yes, w/ random pivot
  Hash map lookup     | O(1)       | O(1)       | O(n)†      | yes, almost always

  * Quicksort hits O(n²) on bad pivot choices — random pivots make it rare.
  † Hash collisions can degrade to a linked list. With good hash + load
    factor, this almost never happens in practice.

sections.title: Why quicksort is 'usually O(n log n) but worst-case O(n²)'

steps.text: Quicksort picks a 'pivot', partitions the array into smaller-than and larger-than, then recurses on both halves.
**Best case**: every pivot lands in the middle. Each recursion level processes all n items, and there are log n levels. → O(n log n).
**Worst case**: every pivot is the smallest (or largest) element. The 'split' degenerates: one side is empty, the other has n-1 items. Now we have n levels, each doing O(n) work. → **O(n²)**.
steps.highlight: Trigger: pre-sorted array + pivoting on first element.

steps.text: **Average case**: over random inputs (or with a random pivot), the splits are 'usually balanced enough'. Math says expected operations are still O(n log n). This is what 'expected case' means.
**Fix**: pick a random pivot, or use median-of-three. This makes the O(n²) case vanishingly rare. That's why real-world quicksort is fine.

sections.heading: Amortized vs average — a subtle distinction
sections.body:
Two close relatives of average case:

**Amortized** — the average cost per operation across a *sequence* of operations. JavaScript's `arr.push()` is **amortized O(1)**: usually fast, occasionally slow when the underlying array has to grow. Over many pushes, the average is constant.

**Expected** — randomness in the algorithm. Quicksort with a random pivot has *expected* O(n log n) over its own random coin flips, regardless of input.

Both give better-than-worst-case guarantees, but for different reasons.

questions.question: When engineers say 'O(n²)' without specifying, they usually mean:
questions.explanation: By convention, Big O without qualification refers to worst-case complexity — it's the safety guarantee.

questions.question: Quicksort's WORST case happens when:
options: The pivot is always the median
The pivot is always the smallest or largest element
questions.explanation: If the pivot keeps being an extreme, partitions are unbalanced (n-1 vs 0), and you get O(n²).

questions.question: JavaScript's `arr.push(x)` is described as 'amortized O(1)'. What does that mean?
options: Most pushes are O(1); occasional ones are O(n), but averaged across many pushes it's O(1) each
It's O(1) only in the best case
It's O(n) but we pretend it's O(1)
questions.explanation: Amortized analysis spreads the rare expensive operation across the many cheap ones.

questions.question: A hash map lookup is O(1) on average, but in the worst case (lots of collisions) it can be O(___).
questions.explanation: Worst-case hash collisions chain into a linked list, making lookup linear.
